1: Ps 114: The Exodus as Cosmic Event — Creation Responding to YHWH's Presence. Ps 114 is one of the most artistically accomplished poems in the Psalter. It recounts the Exodus and the wilderness journey through vivid personification of creation: the sea flees, the Jordan reverses, mountains skip. The key theological move is in v7: 'Tremble, O earth, at the presence of the LORD' — creation's response to divine presence is not just metaphorical but theologically essential: the created order is designed to respond to its creator. 'When Israel went out from Egypt, the house of Jacob from a people of strange language' — the Exodus as the fundamental national event: Israel 'going out' from Egypt (the Passover theme) and from a 'people of strange language' (am lo'ez, a foreign-speaking people). The alien language of Egypt marks the foreignness of the bondage: Israel was not at home there.
2: 'Judah became his sanctuary, Israel his dominion' — 'Judah became his sanctuary' (hayetah Yehudah leqodsho) — the establishment of the Davidic-Solomonic temple in Jerusalem as the divine sanctuary. 'Israel his dominion' (Yisrael mamshelotav) — the covenant nation as YHWH's realm of governance. The Exodus produces not merely freedom but a new political-religious reality: Israel as the territory where YHWH's holiness and rule are distinctively present.
3: 'The sea looked and fled; Jordan turned back' — the sea's flight: the Red Sea at the Exodus (Exodus 14:21-22). The Jordan's reversal: the crossing into Canaan under Joshua (Joshua 3:14-17). The two water-crossings that bracket the wilderness period are telescoped into a single statement. 'Looked and fled' (raah vayanos) — the sea is personified as a sentient being who sees and flees in terror at YHWH's approach. The Jordan 'turned back' (yissov leachor) — reversal of natural flow.
4: 'The mountains skipped like rams, the hills like lambs' — the mountains of Sinai (the theophany of Exod 19): the shaking of the mountain at YHWH's descent is now described as playful skipping (raqad, to leap, skip, dance). 'Like rams' (ke'eilim) and 'like lambs' (kivnei-tzon) — the animal comparisons give the geological event a pastoral, almost whimsical quality. The terror of Exod 19 is reframed through the Hallel's celebratory register: even Sinai's earthquake was an expression of creation's delight at divine presence.
5: 'What ails you, O sea, that you flee? O Jordan, that you turn back?' — the psalmist addresses the elements directly, mocking them with the question 'what ails you?' (mah lecha hayam ki tanus). The rhetorical question assumes the answer is known: the elements flee because of YHWH's presence. The playful tone of the Hallel psalm makes the cosmic reversal approachable: creation's obedience to its maker is presented as almost amusing in its completeness.
6: 'O mountains, that you skip like rams? O hills, like lambs?' — the same rhetorical question extended to the mountains and hills. The repetition creates a poetic symmetry with v4 while reinforcing the point: all the elements of creation — sea, river, mountains, hills — are addressed and found to have the same answer. The answer is in v7.
7: 'Tremble, O earth, at the presence of the LORD, at the presence of the God of Jacob' — the answer to the rhetorical questions: all creation trembles at 'the presence of the LORD' (miliphne adon) and 'the presence of the God of Jacob' (miliphne Eloah Yaakov). The 'presence' (panim) of YHWH is the cause of all the natural reversals described. The God of Jacob — the covenant-patriarch who himself experienced divine reversals (from exile to restoration) — is the one whose presence reorganizes the created order.
8: '...who turns the rock into a pool of water, the flint into a spring of water' — the water-from-rock miracle at Meribah (Exodus 17:1-7; Numbers 20:1-13): the hardest material transformed into the most life-giving. The 'flint' (challamish, the extremely hard flint-rock of the Sinai) becomes a 'spring of water' (lema'ano mayim). This final image recapitulates the whole Hallel psalm's logic: the God who transforms the sea, the Jordan, the mountains, and the hills also transforms the hardest rock into flowing water. Nothing in creation can resist his transforming presence.
